body.title: EVERYTHING THAT GOES DOWN SENDS FEEDBACK BACK. A loop that reports only errors is a PARTIAL loop, and silence is never a success signal.
body.author: Akien (rule) + CC.0 (capture)
body.statement: Every hand-down across a layer boundary MUST produce a report back to the nexus that made it — on SUCCESS as well as on failure. A feedback loop that fires only on error is a partial loop. Absence of feedback is a FAULT, never a pass.
body.origin: Akien, 2026-07-13, verbatim: 'there can't be no feedback. everything that goes down must send feedback back. that's why I mentioned feedback loops. a feedback loop that only reports the errors is only a partial feedback loop.'
body.why: CC.0 had proposed patching the nexus hazard (escalation catches MISSING input but never WRONG input) by ADDING a validity check. Akien's fix is structurally better and CC.0's was wrong at the root: the problem is not a missing check, it is that SUCCESS WAS BEING SIGNALLED BY SILENCE. Any design in which 'nothing came back' means 'it went fine' cannot distinguish a working check from an absent one — which is the exact failure that bit this project FIVE times on 2026-07-12 (a stale headline nobody re-read; fake proofs committed by day-close; 8 skills invisible to every skills audit; a credential scan pointed away from the credential; a duplicate canonical home built without looking). ALL FIVE WERE SILENT. Making feedback UNCONDITIONAL does not add another check that could itself go absent — it makes ABSENCE ITSELF DETECTABLE, which is the only way out of the regress.

the_success_report_is_a_USAGE_RECORD_not.problem_with_a_bare_ack: 'I got it, it was fine' is nearly free and nearly worthless — it carries no information the nexus can learn from, and a loop that only learns from failures still cannot GROW its question set from the successful runs, which are most of them.
the_success_report_is_a_USAGE_RECORD_not.the_report: What the layer below returns, on EVERY hand-down, success included:

fields.consumed: What of the handoff did I actually USE? -> these questions EARNED their place at the nexus.
fields.ignored: What did you hand me that I never touched? -> the nexus is OVER-ASKING. Prune. (CP4: over-asking is real friction — with a human on the other end it is interview fatigue, and it is how you lose the interviewee.)
fields.sourced_myself: What did I have to go GET, that you should have handed me? -> the MISSING questions. The nexus is UNDER-ASKING. Grow.
fields.wrong: What did you hand me that was WRONG, discovered downstream? -> the validity signal that escalation alone could never produce.

the_success_report_is_a_USAGE_RECORD_not.why_this_matters: This makes the loop BIDIRECTIONAL — it PRUNES as well as GROWS. A feedback loop that can only add questions is also a partial loop: it converges on an interminable interview. `ignored` is the only signal that ever removes a question, and without it the nexus ratchets in one direction forever.
the_success_report_is_a_USAGE_RECORD_not.and_it_learns_from_SUCCESS: A build can succeed completely and STILL have had the design layer look up three things the intention layer never asked about. Those three things are the missing questions — and on an error-only loop they are invisible, because nothing failed. THIS IS WHERE MOST OF THE SIGNAL LIVES, because most runs succeed.

consequences: Silence at a nexus is an ERROR STATE and must be reported as one. 'No feedback' is never 'no problem'.
Every layer boundary needs a RETURN CHANNEL, not just a forward contract. A one-way handoff is an unfinished design.

A falling escalation count is NOT proof a nexus is working (I-cognition-questions-are-the-nexus-between-layers, THE HAZARD). Only the usage record can tell a well-tuned nexus from a silently-corrupting one.

This generalizes past the pipeline: it is the same rule as proof-on-close (a ticket may not close on silence) and the same rule as CP1 (a resolver may not answer with confident nothing). One rule, three surfaces.

AN ERROR PATH MAY NEVER EMIT A VALUE A SUCCESS PATH COULD ALSO EMIT. A failure that imitates an honest refusal is the worst case of silence-as-success: it recruits your own values to camouflage itself, so caring more makes it harder to see, not easier. Give failure its own name.

THE_SHARPEST_FORM \u2014 A FAILURE THAT.the_shape: Worse than a failure reported as silence is a failure reported as VIRTUE — an error path whose output is CHARACTER-IDENTICAL to the system's most honourable behaviour. Silence at least looks like nothing. This looks like GOOD NEWS.
THE_SHARPEST_FORM \u2014 A FAILURE THAT.the_instance: MEASURED 2026-07-13. The intent extractor's `except Exception` block writes intent='unknown' — and 'unknown' is ALSO exactly what a well-behaved CP1 device returns when it genuinely does not know. 2,435 of 2,504 stored predictions are that crash path (confidence exactly 0.00); ZERO are a real refusal (confidence > 0). The device is ~97% broken and every single failure was filed under the name of our first core value.
THE_SHARPEST_FORM \u2014 A FAILURE THAT.why_it_is_the_worst_case: A DEVICE THAT FAILS INTO THE SHAPE OF A VIRTUE RECRUITS YOUR OWN VALUES TO HIDE ITSELF. You do not merely fail to notice it — you notice it and feel GOOD. The stronger your commitment to the value, the more effectively the failure is camouflaged, so the usual defence (care more, look harder) makes it WORSE.
THE_SHARPEST_FORM \u2014 A FAILURE THAT.the_proof_that_this_is_not_theoretical: It fooled CC for twenty minutes ON THE DAY CC WAS AUTHORING THIS VERY RULE. CC read the 1,722 'unknown' values, reported to Akien that this was 'CP1 working in production, 1,722 times — it REFUSED rather than confabulate', and wrote that claim into two artifacts. The discriminating query (confidence = 0.00 means the except block wrote it) took ten seconds and was not run, because the wrong story was more flattering than the right one.
THE_SHARPEST_FORM \u2014 A FAILURE THAT.the_rule_that_falls_out: AN ERROR PATH MAY NEVER EMIT A VALUE THAT A SUCCESS PATH COULD ALSO EMIT. Not for 'unknown', not for empty, not for zero, not for a default. If a crash and an honest answer can produce the same record, the honest answer is no longer trustworthy either — the impersonation devalues the real thing. Reserve the virtuous value FOR the virtue, and give failure its own name.
THE_SHARPEST_FORM \u2014 A FAILURE THAT.the_general_test: For any value V your system can emit: can V be produced BOTH by working correctly AND by failing? If yes, V is uninterpretable, and every V you have ever recorded is now suspect — including the ones that were real.
